title: Prove parallelogram properties
description: Use transformations, triangle congruency criteria, and line and angle relationship to prove parallelogram properties.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kun wants to prove that if opposite sides of a quadrilateral are congruent, then it is a parallelogram.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Select the appropriate rephrased statement for Kun's proof.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we set out to prove a conjecture, we need a specific identifier for the quadrilateral we talk about. So we start our rephrased statement with "$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$..."

Now, this is the "if... then" statement we need to rephrase:

If... | Then...
:- | :-
Opposite sides are congruent | It is a parallelogram

How can we express each of these as a statement about quadrilateral $ABCD$?

### Opposite sides are congruent

If the opposite sides of quadrilateral $ABCD$ are congruent, it means that $\maroonD{\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}\text{ and }\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}}$.

### It is a parallelogram

The definition of a parallelogram is a quadrilateral where both pairs of opposite sides are parallel. So saying quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a parallelogram is the same as saying that $\goldE{\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}\text{ and }\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}}$.

In conclusion,  this is the appropriate rephrased statement:

$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$, if $\maroonD{\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}\text{ and }\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}}$, then $\goldE{\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}\text{ and }\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}}$.

options.alt: A Quadrilateral A B C D.

choices.content: In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}$, then $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$, then $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$, then $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{AD}\cong \overline{BC}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vera wants to prove that any rectangle is also a parallelogram.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Select the appropriate rephrased statement for Vera's proof.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we set out to prove a conjecture, we need a specific identifier for the quadrilateral we talk about. So we start our rephrased statement with "$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$..."

Now, this is the "if... then" statement we need to rephrase:

If... | Then...
:- | :-
It is a rectangle | It is a parallelogram

How can we express each of these as a statement about quadrilateral $ABCD$?

### It is a rectangle

The definition of a rectangle is a quadrilateral with four right angles. So saying quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a rectangle is the same as saying that $\maroonD{m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree}$.

### It is a parallelogram

The definition of a parallelogram is a quadrilateral where both pairs of opposite sides are parallel. So saying quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a parallelogram is the same as saying that $\goldE{\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}\text{ and }\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}}$.

In conclusion,  this is the appropriate rephrased statement:

$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$, if $\maroonD{m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree}$, then $\goldE{\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}\text{ and }\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}}$.

options.alt: A Rectangle A B C D.

choices.content: In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$, then $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$, then $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}$, then $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$, then $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Branson wants to prove that the diagonals in a rectangle are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Select the appropriate rephrased statement for Branson's proof.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we set out to prove a conjecture, we need a specific identifier for the quadrilateral we talk about. So we start our rephrased statement with "$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$..."

Now, this is the "if... then" statement we need to rephrase:

If... | Then...
:- | :-
It is a rectangle | Diagonals are congruent

How can we express each of these as a statement about quadrilateral $ABCD$?

### It is a rectangle

The definition of a rectangle is a quadrilateral with four right angles. So saying quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a rectangle is the same as saying that $\maroonD{m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree}$.

### Diagonals are congruent

The diagonals of quadrilateral $ABCD$ are $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$. So saying the diagonals are congruent is the same as saying that $\goldE{\overline{AC}\cong\overline{BD}}$.

In conclusion,  this is the appropriate rephrased statement:

$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$, if $\maroonD{m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree}$, then $\goldE{\overline{AC}\cong\overline{BD}}$.

options.alt: A Rectangle A B C D. A line connects from points A and C. Another line connects points B and D, intersecting in the middle of the rectangle.

choices.content: In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$, then $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$, then $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AC}\cong\overline{BD}$, then $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $m\angle A=m\angle B=m\angle C=m\angle D=90\degree$, then $\overline{AC}\cong\overline{BD}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rebecca wants to prove that if the diagonals in a parallelogram are perpendicular, then it is a rhombus.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Select the appropriate rephrased statement for Rebecca's proof.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we set out to prove a conjecture, we need a specific identifier for the quadrilateral we talk about. So we start our rephrased statement with "$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$..."

Now, this is the "if... then" statement we need to rephrase:

If... | Then...
:- | :-
The diagonals in a parallelogram are perpendicular | It is a rhombus

How can we express each of these as a statement about quadrilateral $ABCD$?

### The diagonals in a parallelogram are perpendicular

First, we need to establish that quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a parallelogram. The definition of a parallelogram is a quadrilateral where both pairs of opposite sides are parallel. So saying quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a parallelogram is the same as saying that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.

The diagonals of quadrilateral $ABCD$ are $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$. So saying the diagonals are perpendicular is the same as saying that $\overline{AC}\perp\overline{BD}$.

Together, the statement is $\maroonD{\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}\text{, }\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}\text{, and }\overline{AC}\perp\overline{BD}}$.

### It is a rhombus

The definition of a rhombus is a quadrilateral with four congruent sides. So saying quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a rhombus is the same as saying that $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{BC}\cong\overline{CD}\cong\overline{DA}$. Alternatively, we can say that $\goldE{AB=BC=CD=DA}$.

In conclusion,  this is the appropriate rephrased statement:

$\blueE{\text{In quadrilateral }ABCD}$, if $\maroonD{\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}\text{, }\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}\text{, and }\overline{AC}\perp\overline{BD}}$, then $\goldE{AB=BC=CD=DA}$.

options.alt: A Parallelogram A B C D. A line connects from points A and C. Another line connects points B and D, intersecting in the middle of the parallelogram.

choices.content: In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$, then $\overline{AC}\perp\overline{BD}$ and $AB=BC=CD=DA$.
In quadrilateral $ABCD$, if $\overline{AC}\perp\overline{BD}$ and $AB=BC=CD=DA$, then $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this diagram of quadrilateral $ABCD$, which is not drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the information the diagram indicates:

- $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DC}$ (they both have a single tick mark)
- $\overline{AD}\cong\overline{BC}$ (they both have double tick marks)

If a statement must be true based on the diagram, it means we can write a proof for it using the above information.

If a statement isn't necessarily true based on the diagram, it means we can come up with a figure that has the same properties, but the statement isn't true.

Let's consider each statement and see whether it must be true or not.

### $\overline{AC}\cong\overline{BD}$

$\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ aren't necessarily congruent. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ are not congruent.

### $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$

This indeed must be true. To see why, consider this proof:

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{CD}$ | Given
2 | $\overline{BC}\cong \overline{DA}$ | Given
3 | $\overline{CA}\cong \overline{AC}$ | Line segments are congruent to themselves.
4 | $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$ | Side-side-side congruence. (1,2,3)

### $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DC}$

The diagram explicitly indicates that $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{DC}$ are congruent. So this indeed must be true.

### $\angle ABD\cong\angle DBC$

$\angle ABD$ and $\angle DBC$ aren't necessarily congruent. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, $m\angle ABD$ is much smaller than $m\angle DBC$.

In conclusion, these two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram:

- $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$
- $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DC}$

options.alt: A Quadrilateral A B C D. A line connects from points A and C. Another line connects points B and D, intersecting in the middle of the rectangle. Side A B and Side D C are congruent. Side A D and side B C are congruent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this diagram of quadrilateral $ABCD$, which is not drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the information the diagram indicates:

- $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ (they both have a single arrow mark)
- $m\angle CDA=90\degree$

If a statement must be true based on the diagram, it means we can write a proof for it using the above information.

If a statement isn't necessarily true based on the diagram, it means we can come up with a figure that has the same properties, but the statement isn't true.

Let's consider each statement and see whether it must be true or not.

### $m\angle DAB=90\degree$

This indeed must be true. To see why, consider this proof:

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ | Given
2 | $m\angle CDA=90\degree$ | Given
3 | $m\angle CDA+m\angle DAB=180\degree$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, same-side interior angles sum up to $180\degree$.
4 | $90\degree+m\angle DAB=180\degree$ | Substitute (2,3)
5 | $m\angle DAB=90\degree$ | Subtract $90\degree$ from both sides (4)

### $m\angle ABC=90\degree$

$m\angle ABC$ isn't necessarily $90\degree$. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, $m\angle ABC$ is greater than $90\degree$.

### $\angle DAC\cong\angle BCA$

$\angle DAC$ and $\angle BCA$ aren't necessarily congruent. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, $\overline{AD}$ and $\overline{BC}$ are not parallel and therefore $\angle DAC$ and $\angle BCA$ are not congruent.

### $\angle ABD\cong\angle CDB$

This indeed must be true. To see why, consider this proof:

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{DC}$ | Given
2 | $\angle ABD\cong\angle CDB$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent.

In conclusion, these two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram:

- $m\angle DAB=90\degree$
- $\angle ABD\cong\angle CDB$

options.alt: A quadrilateral A B C D. Side A B is parallel to side C D. Angle A D C is ninety degrees. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this diagram of quadrilateral $ABCD$, which is not drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the information the diagram indicates:

- $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$  (they both have a single arrow mark)
- $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ (they both have double arrow marks)
- $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DA}$ (they both have a single tick mark)

If a statement must be true based on the diagram, it means we can write a proof for it using the above information.

If a statement isn't necessarily true based on the diagram, it means we can come up with a figure that has the same properties, but the statement isn't true.

Let's consider each statement and see whether it must be true or not.

### $ABCD$ is a parallelogram

The definition of a parallelogram is a quadrilateral where both pairs of opposite sides are parallel. Since we are given that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$, quadrilateral $ABCD$ is indeed a parallelogram.

### $ABCD$ is a rectangle

$ABCD$ isn't necessarily a rectangle. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, none of the angles are right angles. Since the definition of a rectangle is a quadrilateral with four right angles, $ABCD$ is not a rectangle.

### $ABCD$ is a rhombus

This indeed must be true. To see why, consider this proof, which uses the following theorem: *Opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent*.

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{DA}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{DA}\cong\overline{BC}$ | Opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent.
3 | $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{DA}$ | Given
4 | $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{BC}$ | Substitute (2,3)
5 | $ABCD$ is a rhombus. | A rhombus is a quadrilateral with four congruent sides. (2,3,4)

### $ABCD$ is a square

$ABCD$ isn't necessarily a square. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, none of the angles are right angles. Since the definition of a square is a quadrilateral with four right angles (and four congruent sides), $ABCD$ is not a square.

In conclusion, these two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram:

- $ABCD$ is a parallelogram.
- $ABCD$ is a rhombus.

options.alt: A quadrilateral A B C D. Side A B is parallel to side C D. Side A D is parallel to side B C. Side A B is congruent to side A D. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this diagram of quadrilateral $ABCD$, which is not drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the information the diagram indicates:

- $\overline{AE}\cong\overline{CE}$ (they both have a single tick mark)
- $\overline{BE}\cong\overline{DE}$ (they both have double tick marks)

If a statement must be true based on the diagram, it means we can write a proof for it using the above information.

If a statement isn't necessarily true based on the diagram, it means we can come up with a figure that has the same properties, but the statement isn't true.

Let's consider each statement and see whether it must be true or not.

### $\angle CDE\cong\angle ECD$

$\angle CDE$ isn't necessarily congruent to $\angle ECD$. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, $m\angle CDE$ is smaller than $m\angle ECD$.

### $\angle ABE\cong\angle CDE$

This indeed must be true. To see why, consider this proof:

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AE}\cong\overline{CE}$ | Given
2 | $\overline{BE}\cong\overline{DE}$ | Given
3 | $\angle BEA\cong\angle DEC$ | Vertical angles are congruent.
4 | $\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE$ | Side-angle-side congruence (1,3,2)
5 | $\angle ABE\cong\angle CDE$ | Corresponding angles in congruent triangles are congruent. (4)

### $\overline{AC}\cong\overline{BD}$

$\overline{AC}$ isn't necessarily congruent to $\overline{BD}$. To see why, consider the following diagram drawn to scale.



[[☃ image 1]]

While the information is the same, $AC$ is smaller than $BD$.

### $\overline{AE}\cong\overline{CE}$

The diagram explicitly indicates that $\overline{AE}$ and $\overline{CE}$ are congruent. So this indeed must be true.

In conclusion, these two statements must be true based on the information indicated by the diagram:

- $\angle ABE\cong\angle CDE$
- $\overline{AE}\cong\overline{CE}$

options.alt: Quadrilateral A B C D. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D. These lines intersect at a point E. Segment A E is congruent to segment C E. Segment B E is congruent to segment D E.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alva wants to prove that opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

Alva says: "I can prove this by establishing the congruence of a single pair of triangles."

**Which pair of triangles is Alva referring to, and which criterion should she use for establishing congruence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The options give us two pairs of triangles to consider:

- $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$
- $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CDE$

Let's start by asking ourselves which pair can we use to prove that opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent.

Then we can also determine which criterion can be used to do that.

If we know that $\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE$, we can conclude that $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{CD}$, but we can't conclude the same for $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{DA}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

However, if we know that $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$, then we can conclude that $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{CD}$ *and* $\overline{BC}\cong\overline{DA}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So what criterion can be used to establish $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$?

They have a shared side, $\overline{AC}$, and we can prove the congruence of their corresponding angles using the fact that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{BC}\parallel\overline{DA}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So the angle-side-angle criterion should do it!
In conclusion, Alva can prove that opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent by establishing the congruence of **$\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$ by angle-side-angle**.

options.alt: A quadrilateral A B C D. Side A B is parallel to side C D. Side A D is parallel to side B C. Side A B is congruent to side A D. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D. The lines intersect at a point E.

assessment_items.question_content:
Craig wants to prove that if quadrilateral $ABCD$ has diagonals that bisect each other, then it is a parallelogram.



[[☃ image 1]]

Craig says: "I can prove that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ by establishing the congruence of a single pair of triangles."

**Which pair of triangles is Craig referring to, and which criterion should he use for establishing congruence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The options give us two pairs of triangles to consider:

- $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$
- $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CDE$

Let's start by asking ourselves which pair can we use to prove that $\overline{AB}$ is parallel to $\overline{CD}$.

Then we can also determine which criterion can be used to do that.

If we know that $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$, then we can conclude that $\angle CAB\cong\angle ACD$ which means $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ are parallel.



[[☃ image 1]]

However, the diagram doesn't give us enough information to establish that $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$. At least not without establishing the congruence of other triangles first.

If we know that $\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE$, then we can again conclude that $\angle CAB\cong\angle ACD$ which means $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ are parallel.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can also establish the congruence of $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CDE$ with the information given by the diagram. What criterion can be used for that?

We are given information about the corresponding congruent pairs $\overline{AE}\cong\overline{CE}$ and $\overline{BE}\cong\overline{DE}$, and we can prove the congruence of the included angles using the fact that vertical angles are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

So the side-angle-side criterion should do it!
In conclusion, Craig can prove that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ by establishing the congruence of **$\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CDE$ by side-angle-side**.

options.alt: A quadrilateral A B C D.  There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D. The lines intersect at a point E. Segment D E is congruent to segment B E. Segment A E is congruent to segment C E.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alessandra wants to prove that if quadrilateral $ABCD$ has one pair of congruent and parallel opposite sides, then it is a parallelogram.



[[☃ image 1]]

Alessandra says: "I can prove this by establishing the congruence of a single pair of triangles."

**Which pair of triangles is Alessandra referring to, and which criterion should she use for establishing congruence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that we already know that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$. To prove that $ABCD$ is a parallelogram, we need to establish that $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.

The options give us two pairs of triangles to consider:

- $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$
- $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CDE$

Let's start by asking ourselves which pair can we use to prove that $\overline{AD}$ is parallel to $\overline{BC}$.

Then we can also determine which criterion can be used to do that.

If we know that $\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE$, there's nothing we can use in order to show that $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

However, if we know that $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$, then we can conclude that $\angle BCA\cong\angle DAC$ which means $\overline{AD}$ and $\overline{BC}$ are parallel.



[[☃ image 1]]

So what criterion can be used to establish $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$?

We are given that $\overline{AB}\cong\overline{CD}$, and $\overline{AC}\cong\overline{CA}$ because they are the same segment. We can prove that $\angle CAB \cong \angle ACD$ using the fact that $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So the side-angle-side criterion should do it!

In conclusion, Alessandra can prove that if quadrilateral $ABCD$ has one pair of congruent and parallel opposite sides, then it is a parallelogram, by establishing the congruence of **$\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$ by side-angle-side**.

options.alt: A quadrilateral A B C D. Side A B is parallel to side C D. Side A B is congruent to side C D. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D. They intersect at point E.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bao wants to prove that if parallelogram $ABCD$ has a right angle, then its diagonals are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

Bao says: "I can prove this using the fact that opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent, and by establishing the congruence of a single pair of triangles."

**Which pair of triangles is Bao referring to, and which criterion should he use for establishing congruence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The options give us two pairs of triangles to consider:

- $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle CDA$
- $\triangle CDA$ and $\triangle DCB$

Let's start by asking ourselves which pair can we use to prove that diagonals in a parallelogram with a right angle are congruent.

Then we can also determine which criterion can be used to do that.

If we know that $\triangle ABC\cong\triangle CDA$, we can't conclude anything about diagonal $\overline{BD}$ because it's not a side in any of the triangles.



[[☃ image 1]]

However, if we know that $\triangle CDA\cong\triangle DCB$, then we can conclude that diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

So what criterion can be used to establish $\triangle CDA\cong\triangle DCB$?

They have a shared side ($\overline{CD}$), the sides $\overline{AD}$ and $\overline{BC}$ are congruent because they are opposite sides in a parallelogram, and we can prove the congruence of the included angles using the fact that $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ and $m\angle CDA=90\degree$.


[[☃ image 1]]

So the side-angle-side criterion should do it!

In conclusion, Bao can prove that if parallelogram $ABCD$ has a right angle, then its diagonals are congruent, by establishing the congruence of **$\triangle CDA$ and $\triangle DCB$ by side-angle-side**.

options.alt: Parallelogram A B C D. Angle D is a right angle. Side A B is parallel to side D C. Side A D is parallel to side B C. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D. These lines intersect at a point E.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the proof that the opposite angles of parallelogram $ABCD$ are congruent.**



[[☃ image 1]]

This will prove that $\angle B\cong\angle D$. We can use a similar proof for the other pair of opposite angles by using the diagonal $\overline{BD}$ instead of $\overline{AC}$.

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\angle 2\cong \angle 4$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, [[☃ dropdown 1]] angles are congruent.
3 | $\angle 1\cong \angle 3$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent.
4 | [[☃ dropdown 2]] | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
5 | $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$ | Angle-side-angle congruence (2,4,3)
6 | $\angle B\cong \angle D$ | Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent (5).
hints:
####Writing a proof

A **proof** is a formal description of how we can know that something is true based on the information from a mathematical scenario.

- Each step of the proof is a transformation, a construction, or a statement about what we can conclude from earlier steps.
- For a **two-column proof** we give a reason to justify each statement. Common reasons include geometric theorems, algebraic properties, and information given in diagrams.

### Step 2

$\angle 2$ and $\angle 4$ are **alternate interior angles** formed by the transversal $\overline{AC}$ crossing parallel line segments $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$.

### Step 4

In step 5 we want to establish that $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$ using angle-side-angle congruence. In steps 2 and 3 we establish the congruence of two pairs of corresponding angles. So in step 4 we need to establish the congruence of a pair of corresponding sides.

The sides included between the congruence angles are $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{CA}$, which are one and the same.

So an appropriate statement for step 4 would be $AC=CA$ and an appropriate reason would be that **they are lengths of the same segment.**

### Completed proof

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\angle 2\cong \angle 4$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, $\blueE{\text{alternate interior}}$ angles are congruent.
3 | $\angle 1\cong \angle 3$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent.
4 | $\maroonE{AC=CA}$ | $\goldE{\text{They are lengths of the same segment.}}$
5 | $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$ | Angle-side-angle congruence (2,4,3)
6 | $\angle B\cong \angle D$ | Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent (5).

choices.content: They are corresponding sides between congruent triangles.
They are lengths of the same segment.

options.alt: Parallelogram A B C D. Side A B is parallel to side D C. Side A D is parallel to side B C. There is one line from point A to point C.   Angle D A C is labeled one. Angle B A C is labeled two. Angle A C B is labeled three. Angle A C D is labeled four.

assessment_items.question_content:
In the diagram below, $\overline{AC}\cong \overline{DB}$.

**Complete the proof that a parallelogram whose diagonals are congruent has a right angle.**



[[☃ image 1]]

This proof uses this theorem: *Opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent*.

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}$ | Opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent. (1)
3 | $\overline{AD}\cong \overline{DA}$ | Line segments are congruent to themselves.
4 | $\overline{AC}\cong \overline{DB}$ | Given
5 | [[☃ dropdown 3]] | Side-side-side congruence (2,3,4)
6 | [[☃ dropdown 1]] | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
7 | $m\angle 1+m\angle 4=180\degree$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, same-side interior angles sum up to $180\degree$. (1)
8 | $2\cdot m\angle 1=180\degree$ | Substitute (6,7)
9 | $m\angle 1=90\degree$ | Divide both sides by $2$ (8)
hints:
####Writing a proof

A **proof** is a formal description of how we can know that something is true based on the information from a mathematical scenario.

- Each step of the proof is a transformation, a construction, or a statement about what we can conclude from earlier steps.
- For a **two-column proof** we give a reason to justify each statement. Common reasons include geometric theorems, algebraic properties, and information given in diagrams.

### Step 5

- In step 2 we established $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}$.
- In step 3 we established $\overline{AD}\cong \overline{DA}$.
- In step 4 we established $\overline{AC}\cong \overline{DB}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So in step 5 we can claim that $\triangle CDA\cong\triangle BAD$ using side-side-side congruence.

### Step 6

To figure out the appropriate statement for step 6, we need to look up ahead in the proof. In step 8, we combine step 6 with the fact that $m\angle 1+m\angle 4=180\degree$ (step 7) to claim that $2\cdot m\angle 1=180\degree$.

In order to make such a conclusion, step 6 must claim that $m\angle 1=m\angle 4$.

The appropriate reason for that is the fact that they are corresponding parts in congruent triangles $\triangle CDA$ and $\triangle BAD$ (step 5).

### Completed proof

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{DC}$ | Opposite sides in a parallelogram are congruent. (1)
3 | $\overline{AD}\cong \overline{DA}$ | Line segments are congruent to themselves.
4 | $\overline{AC}\cong \overline{DB}$ | Given
5 | $\blueE{\triangle CDA\cong\triangle BAD}$ | Side-side-side congruence (2,3,4)
6 | $\maroonE{m\angle 1=m\angle 4}$ | $\begin{align}&\goldE{\text{Corresponding parts of congruent }}\\&\goldE{\text{triangles are congruent. (5)}}\end{align}$
7 | $m\angle 1+m\angle 4=180\degree$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, same-side interior angles sum up to $180\degree$. (1)
8 | $2\cdot m\angle 1=180\degree$ | Substitute (6,7)
9 | $m\angle 1=90\degree$ | Divide both sides by $2$ (8)

choices.content: Opposite angles in a parallelogram are congruent.
Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent. (5)
When a transversal crosses parallel lines, same-side interior angles sum up to 180°.

options.alt: Parallelogram A B C D. Side A B is parallel to side D C. Side A D is parallel to side B C. There is one line from point A to point C. There is another line from point B to point D. These lines intersect at a point E. Segment B E is congruent to segment C E. Angle D A B is labeled one. Angle C B A is labeled two. Angle D C B is labeled three. Angle A D C is labeled four. Angle 1, Angle 2, Angle 3 , and Angle 4 have angle symbols.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the proof that the diagonals of parallelogram $ABCD$ bisect each other.**



[[☃ image 1]]

This proof uses the following theorem: *Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent.*

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\angle 1\cong \angle 3$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent. (1)
3 | $\angle 2\cong \angle 4$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent. (1)
4 | $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{CD}$ | Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent. (1)
5 | [[☃ dropdown 1]] | [[☃ dropdown 2]] congruence
6 | [[☃ dropdown 3]] | Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent. (5)
7 | Point $E$ bisects both $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ | Definition of bisector (6)
hints:
####Writing a proof

A **proof** is a formal description of how we can know that something is true based on the information from a mathematical scenario.

- Each step of the proof is a transformation, a construction, or a statement about what we can conclude from earlier steps.
- For a **two-column proof** we give a reason to justify each statement. Common reasons include geometric theorems, algebraic properties, and information given in diagrams.

### Step 5

- In step 2 we established $\angle 1\cong \angle 3$.
- In step 3 we established $\angle 2\cong \angle 4$.
- In step 4 we established $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{CD}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

So in step 5 we can claim that $\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE$ using angle-side-angle congruence.

### Step 6

In step 5 we showed that $\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE$. In step 6 we want to use that to establish congruence of two pairs of corresponding sides.

The fact that the corresponding pair $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ is congruent is already established and was used to establish the congruence of the triangles.

The other two corresponding pairs are:

- $\overline{AE}$ and $\overline{CE}$
- $\overline{BE}$ and $\overline{DE}$

### Completed proof

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $\angle 1\cong \angle 3$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent. (1)
3 | $\angle 2\cong \angle 4$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent. (1)
4 | $\overline{AB}\cong \overline{CD}$ | Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent. (1)
5 | $\blueE{\triangle ABE\cong\triangle CDE}$ | $\maroonE{\text{Angle-side-angle}}$ congruence
6 | $\goldE{AE=CE\text{ and }BE=DE}$ | Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent. (5)
7 | Point $E$ bisects both $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ | Definition of bisector (6)

options.alt: Parallelogram A B C D. Angle D is a right angle. Side A B is parallel to side D C. Side A D is parallel to side B C. There is one line from point A to point C and another line from point B to point D. These lines intersect at a point E. Angle B A E is labeled one. Angle A B E is labeled two. Angle D C E is labeled three. Angle C D E is labeled four.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the proof that a parallelogram with a right angle is a rectangle.**



[[☃ image 1]]

This proof uses this theorem: *Opposite angles in parallelograms are congruent*.

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $m\angle A=90\degree$ | Given
3 | [[☃ dropdown 1]] | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, [[☃ dropdown 2]]
4 | $90\degree+m\angle D=180\degree$ | Substitute (2,3)
5 | $m\angle D=90\degree$ | Subtract $90\degree$ from both sides (4)
6 | $m\angle C=m\angle A=90\degree$ | Opposite angles in parallelograms are congruent.
7 | $m\angle B=m\angle D=90\degree$ | Opposite angles in parallelograms are congruent.
8 | $ABCD$ is a rectangle. | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints:
####Writing a proof

A **proof** is a formal description of how we can know that something is true based on the information from a mathematical scenario.

- Each step of the proof is a transformation, a construction, or a statement about what we can conclude from earlier steps.
- For a **two-column proof** we give a reason to justify each statement. Common reasons include geometric theorems, algebraic properties, and information given in diagrams.

### Step 3

To figure out the appropriate statement for step 3, we need to look up ahead in the proof. In step 4, we combine step 3 with the fact that $m\angle A=90\degree$ (step 2) to claim that $90\degree+m\angle D=180\degree$.

In order to make such a conclusion, step 3 must claim that $m\angle A+m\angle D=180\degree$.

The appropriate reason for that is the fact that when a transversal crosses parallel lines ($\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$), same-side interior angles sum up to $180\degree$.

### Step 8

In step 8 we claim that $ABCD$ is a rectangle. The justification for that should use the definition of what a rectangle is: *a quadrilateral with four right angles*.

### Completed proof

 | Statement | Reason
- | - | -
1 | $\overline{AB}\parallel\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD}\parallel\overline{BC}$ | Given
2 | $m\angle A=90\degree$ | Given
3 | $\blueE{m\angle A + m\angle D=180 \degree}$ | When a transversal crosses parallel lines, $\begin{align}&\maroonE{\text{same-side interior}}\\&\maroonE{\text{angles sum up to }180\degree\text{.}}\end{align}$
4 | $90\degree+m\angle D=180\degree$ | Substitute (2,3)
5 | $m\angle D=90\degree$ | Subtract $90\degree$ from both sides (4)
6 | $m\angle C=m\angle A=90\degree$ | Opposite angles in parallelograms are congruent.
7 | $m\angle B=m\angle D=90\degree$ | Opposite angles in parallelograms are congruent.
8 | $ABCD$ is a rectangle. | $\goldE{\text{It has four right angles.}}$

choices.content: same-side interior angles sum up to 180°.

options.alt: Parallelogram A B C D. Angle A is a right angle. Side A B is parallel to side D C. Side A D is parallel to side B C.
